prompt:
Every service in our worker fleet has its own copy-pasted retry loop and each one is wrong in its own special way (one sleeps after the last failure, one wraps the real exception in a RuntimeError, one retries KeyboardInterrupt, I could go on). I'm consolidating: one decorator, in `retrying.py`, and the copies get deleted.

What I want:

`retry(attempts, backoff, retry_on=(Exception,), give_up_on=(), sleep=time.sleep, on_retry=None)` — a decorator factory, used as `@retry(...)`. `attempts` is TOTAL call attempts (3 attempts = at most 2 sleeps); attempts < 1 is a ValueError right at decoration, not at call time. `backoff` is any callable mapping the 1-based retry index to a delay in seconds. `sleep` is injectable because our tests (and yours) must never actually sleep. On final failure re-raise the actual last exception object — no wrapping, no losing the original. Never sleep after the last attempt. An exception only triggers a retry if it isinstance-matches `retry_on` — and `give_up_on` overrides retry_on when both match (we use that for auth failures: they're IOErrors but retrying them locks the account). `on_retry(exc, retry_index, delay)` gets called before each sleep so we can hook metrics in. Use functools.wraps — stack traces with `wrapper` in them make me sad. Return values and args pass through exactly.

Also ship the two policies we actually use, as plain callables: `fixed(delay)` (delay >= 0, negative is ValueError) and `exponential(initial, multiplier=2.0, max_delay=None)` (initial > 0, multiplier >= 1, else ValueError; delay = initial * multiplier**(i-1), capped at max_delay when given).

`python3 test_retrying.py` is the gate. Tests stay untouched. Stdlib only.
